---
title: "Architectural feedback on unsum"
author: "Claude Sonnet 4.6 (generated 2026-03-01)"
output: rmarkdown::github_document
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)
```

## Files read

`R/generate.R`, `R/generate-basic.R`, `R/doc-helpers.R`, `R/fn-factory.R`,
`R/fn-formals.R`, `R/read-write.R`, `R/read-write-basic.R`, `R/utils.R`,
`R/plot-bar.R`, `R/plot-bar-basic.R`, `R/plot-ecdf-basic.R`, `R/horns.R`,
`R/constants.R`, `tests/testthat/test-generate.R`, and the S7 HTML docs under
`special-scripts/s7-docs/`.

---

## Overall architecture

The package is built around a single core pattern: a "generator" function
(`closure_generate()`, `sprite_generate()`) returns a named list of five tibbles
(`inputs`, `metrics_main`, `metrics_horns`, `frequency`, `results`), and every
downstream function (`closure_plot_bar()`, `closure_write()`, etc.) consumes
that list.

The main structural layers are:

| Layer | Files | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Core algorithm | `generate-basic.R` | `generate_from_mean_sd_n()`, the one function that actually calls the Rust code |
| Function factory | `fn-factory.R` | `new_generator_mean_sd_n()` etc. build public API functions programmatically |
| Formals DSL | `fn-formals.R` | Manipulation of pairlists to share argument signatures |
| I/O | `read-write-basic.R`, `read-write.R` | Write/read results as Parquet files |
| Validation | `utils.R` | `check_generator_output()`, plus many smaller checks |
| Documentation | `doc-helpers.R` | `expand_section()` generates roxygen sections at build time |

---

## What works well

### The function-factory pattern

`new_generator_mean_sd_n()`, `new_writer_fn()`, `new_reader_fn()`, and
`new_plot_fn_bar()` in `fn-factory.R` cleanly eliminate copy-paste between
technique-specific functions. Adding a third technique would mean registering it
in the factory — no new code per exported function. That is good design.

### `generate_from_mean_sd_n()` as a single source of truth

All technique-specific generator functions delegate to one implementation. The
`technique` argument gates the small differences. This is easier to maintain
than separate per-technique functions would be.

### Separation of in-memory vs. disk mode

The `in_memory_mode` flag in `generate_from_mean_sd_n()` cleanly bifurcates the
two very different execution paths. The logic is easy to follow.

### Thorough validation

`check_generator_output()` (~230 lines in `utils.R`) validates types, shapes,
column names, cross-field constraints (frequencies summing to 1), and the
reading-class pattern. It catches a wide class of user errors.

### The `fn-formals.R` mini-library

The formals DSL (`formals_add()`, `formals_remove()`, `formals_add_defaults()`,
etc.) is well-written and internally documented. It is the only thing making the
function-factory approach viable, since the factory must compose argument lists
at build time rather than repeat them.

---

## What is worth improving (independent of S7)

### 1. The S3 class is on the wrong object

The S3 class that identifies the output type is attached to `data$inputs`, not
to `data` itself:

```r
# generate-basic.R, line ~279:
class = technique |> tolower() |> paste0("_generate")
# applied to data$inputs (a 1×8 tibble), not to the outer list
```

As a result every internal check must write `inherits(data$inputs,
"closure_generate")` rather than `inherits(data, "closure_generate")`.

**Your objection (printing).** Fair point: a classed list without a `print`
method prints as a plain list followed by:

```
attr(,"class")
[1] "closure_generate" "list"
```

That is ugly. But it is also easily fixed: define `print.closure_generate`.
Once R can dispatch to a named method it never falls back to showing the raw
attribute. A minimal implementation:

```r
print.closure_generate <- function(x, ...) {
  print(unclass(x), ...)
  invisible(x)
}
```

That prints exactly what users see today, with no `attr(,"class")` line. A
richer version could add a header line such as `<CLOSURE results: 1,248 samples>`
before the tibbles.

**Your objection (`inherits()` / `is()` / `UseMethod()` are dev tools).** Fully
agreed — users will not call these on package output. The real beneficiary is
the internal code. `check_generator_output()` and `write_basic()` both have to
reach into `data$inputs` just to establish what kind of object `data` is, which
is non-obvious to a contributor reading either function. Putting the class on
the list itself is a code-clarity win, not a user-facing one.

**Does S7 help with printing?** Yes, in the sense that S7 objects have a
default `print` generic and R will call a registered `method(print,
ClosureResult)` automatically — no `attr(,"class")` noise. But this is not a
meaningful advantage over defining `print.closure_generate` in S3. Both solve
the printing problem with one short function.

### 2. The reading-class pattern is fragile

Classes like `"closure_read_include_stats_only"` are stored as strings inside
the `inputs` tibble's class vector and then parsed back with regex:

```r
sub(
  pattern = paste0("^", lowtech, "_read_include_"),
  replacement = "",
  x = reading_class
)
```

**Your objection.** You ask: wouldn't even a cleaned-up version still need to
parse the string to recover the technique and the `include` variant?

The technique is not the issue — every function that calls
`check_generator_output()` already has `technique` (or `lowtech`) in scope as
a parameter. You never need to extract it from the class string. The `include`
variant is the only thing being parsed, and for that, a direct `inherits()`
call is cleaner than regex:

```r
# Instead of regex-parsing a class string out of data$inputs, check the
# top-level list directly (after moving the class there):
if (inherits(data, paste0(lowtech, "_read_include_stats_only"))) {
  # ...
} else if (inherits(data, paste0(lowtech, "_read_include_stats_and_horns"))) {
  # ...
}
```

This still concatenates strings, but it is _checking_ rather than _parsing_:
you are asking "does this object have this class?" rather than "extract the
suffix and treat it as a value." The distinction matters because `inherits()`
is a primitive predicate with well-defined failure modes, while regex parsing on
a class vector is not.

That said, you raised this in the context of point 3 (structural variants),
which is the more pressing concern. See there for a fuller treatment.

### 3. `TIBBLE_NAMES_POSSIBLE_FORMS` signals an unmodeled variant

```r
TIBBLE_NAMES_POSSIBLE_FORMS <- list(
  TIBBLE_NAMES,                                              # 5 elements
  c(TIBBLE_NAMES, "directory"),                             # 6 elements
  c(TIBBLE_NAMES[!TIBBLE_NAMES == "results"], "directory")  # 5 elements
)
```

**Your context.** You described the actual design pressure behind this: loading
all samples into memory is sometimes infeasible (hundreds of millions of values,
potentially hundreds of MB for the `sample` column alone), so `include` controls
how much is read. You experimented with `NA` placeholders but found them
unacceptable:

- A `sample` column of `NA_real_` for 10 M rows costs 80 MB — as much as the
  real data would.
- A single-row NA tibble for `"stats_only"` wastes space proportional to the
  number of samples and adds no information.

This is a real constraint. The three structural forms are not an accident; they
are the deliberate result of a memory-management feature that should be kept.
The question is only how to model them cleanly.

**Why a zero-row sentinel won't work.** A zero-row `results` tibble with the
full schema would be type-consistent, but it conflates two meanings that need to
stay distinct: "no results were found" (genuinely empty run) versus "results
exist on disk but were not loaded." Downstream code already handles the former
as a special case; merging the two signals would break that.

**The actual problem is that the variant is encoded in the wrong place.** The
`include` variant is currently written into the class of `data$inputs` (a nested
tibble) and then decoded with regex. This is fragile because the class is far
from the list element it describes (`results`), and it requires string parsing
rather than type-level inspection. Two concrete alternatives:

**Alternative A — put the variant class on the top-level list (S3, minimal
change).** After moving the main class to the list (point 1), encode the `include`
variant there too:

```r
class(out) <- c(
  paste0(lowtech, "_read_include_", include),  # e.g. "closure_read_include_stats_only"
  paste0(lowtech, "_generate"),
  "list"
)
```

`check_generator_output()` then dispatches with `inherits(data, ...)` rather
than extracting a suffix from a nested tibble's class vector. The regex is gone.
The structural forms are still three, but the code that branches on them is
simpler. `TIBBLE_NAMES_POSSIBLE_FORMS` can be replaced by two `inherits()`
predicates.

**Alternative B — S7 subclasses (clean model, bigger refactor).** This is
actually the strongest concrete argument for S7 in this codebase, stronger than
the class-placement issue. With S7 you model each variant as a distinct class
with only the properties it actually contains:

```r
# Base: stats only (what closure_read(..., include = "stats_only") returns)
ClosureResultStats <- new_class("ClosureResultStats",
  properties = list(
    inputs        = class_data.frame,
    metrics_main  = class_data.frame,
    metrics_horns = class_data.frame,
    frequency     = class_data.frame,
    directory     = class_data.frame
  )
)

# Adds horns column to results (include = "stats_and_horns")
ClosureResultHorns <- new_class("ClosureResultHorns",
  parent = ClosureResultStats,
  properties = list(results = class_data.frame)  # id + horns only
)

# Full results (in-memory generate, or include = "all")
ClosureResultFull <- new_class("ClosureResultFull",
  parent = ClosureResultHorns
  # results inherits from parent; sample column presence is checked in validator
)
```

Each subclass has a tight validator. No nullable properties. No `NULL`
placeholders. No sentinel tibbles. `check_generator_output()` is replaced by
the validators, which run automatically. `method(closure_plot_bar,
ClosureResultStats) <- ...` and `method(closure_plot_bar, ClosureResultFull)
<- ...` can behave differently without any `if/switch` inside the function
body. This is the correct model for the domain.

The cost is the refactor involved. But if the structural variants are causing
ongoing complexity in `check_generator_output()` — which they clearly are — S7
subclasses are the principled solution, not a workaround.

### 4. `check_generator_output()` is doing the job of a class validator

At 230 lines of imperative logic, this function duplicates what a proper class
validator would express declaratively. Every time the output structure changes,
this function must be manually updated. If you forget to call it at a new call
site, the invariants are unguarded. This is not a crisis, but it is the main
structural argument for S7 (see below).

### 5. `doc-helpers.R`: clever but fragile

`expand_section()` runs inside roxygen2's inline-R evaluation at build time.
This creates a hidden dependency: the package must be partially loadable before
its docs can be built. If `expand_section()` errors, documentation breaks
silently. Contributors who don't know to look here will be confused.

The function avoids duplicating text between CLOSURE and SPRITE. But as the
number of techniques grows and they diverge in behavior, `expand_section()` will
grow conditional branches. At some point the indirection cost exceeds the
de-duplication benefit. For now it is acceptable.

---

## Should you use S7 for the output of generator functions?

### What S7 would give you

With S7, `closure_generate()` could return a first-class object:

```r
ClosureResult <- new_class(
  "ClosureResult",
  properties = list(
    inputs        = class_data.frame,
    metrics_main  = class_data.frame,
    metrics_horns = class_data.frame,
    frequency     = class_data.frame,
    results   = new_property(class_data.frame | class_null, default = NULL),
    directory = new_property(class_data.frame | class_null, default = NULL)
  ),
  validator = function(self) {
    # ... the logic currently in check_generator_output()
  }
)
```

Key benefits:

- The class is on the right object (the list, not a nested tibble).
- `inherits(data, "ClosureResult")` works naturally.
- The validator runs automatically on every construction, not only where you
  remember to call `check_generator_output()`.
- Property type declarations are documentation: anyone reading the class
  definition immediately knows the output structure.
- Downstream functions can register S7 methods instead of checking inside the
  function body:
  ```r
  method(closure_plot_bar, ClosureResult) <- function(data, ...) { ... }
  ```
- `$` access still works on S7 objects (`data$results`), so the user-facing API
  is unchanged.

### What S7 would cost you

**Optional fields are awkward.** `results` and `directory` are conditionally
present. In S7 you model these with nullable properties (`class_data.frame |
class_null`). This works but makes the type signature looser than ideal. The
alternative is subclasses:

```r
ClosureResultFull     <- new_class("ClosureResultFull",     parent = ClosureResult)
ClosureResultStats    <- new_class("ClosureResultStats",    parent = ClosureResult)
ClosureResultFromDisk <- new_class("ClosureResultFromDisk", parent = ClosureResult)
```

Subclasses make the `include`-variant logic explicit, which is good, but they
add three new public-facing names and increase the cognitive load of the factory.

**The function-factory pattern becomes harder to compose with S7.** Currently
`new_generator_mean_sd_n("CLOSURE")` and `new_generator_mean_sd_n("SPRITE")`
produce two functions with the same logic. With S7, you need two classes
(`ClosureResult`, `SpriteResult`) and two validators. The factory must now know
about these classes, coupling two concerns that are currently separate.

**`@` vs `$` in documentation.** S7 objects support `$` for property access,
so `data$results` continues to work. But the canonical accessor shown in S7
docs and error messages is `@`. Users unfamiliar with S7 will encounter a new
symbol.

**S7 dependency.** S7 is stable and on CRAN, but it adds a dependency to a
package that currently relies only on well-established packages (rlang, cli,
ggplot2, nanoparquet, etc.).

**The `technique` string dispatch would still exist.** Many internal functions
switch on `technique` to branch between CLOSURE-specific and SPRITE-specific
code paths. S7 method dispatch can replace some of these, but not all — some
branches are in non-generic code (e.g., `closure_read()` vs. `sprite_read()`).
Full replacement of string switching with S7 dispatch would require a more
comprehensive redesign than just adding class definitions.

### The honest comparison

| Concern | S3 with fixes | S7 |
|---|---|---|
| Class on the right object | Fixable: add class to list | Yes, naturally |
| Optional fields | Named constant + length check | Nullable props or subclasses |
| Validation | Explicit call to `check_generator_output()` | Automatic on construction |
| Method dispatch | `switch(technique, ...)` inside functions | `method(generic, Class) <-` |
| `inherits(data, "closure_generate")` | Works after fix | Works out of the box |
| `$` access for users | No change | Still works |
| Breaking changes | Moving class to list | Moving class to list + `@` convention |
| Contributor learning curve | Familiar S3 | New system for most R devs |
| Dependency | None added | S7 |
| Print/format/[ methods | Add `print.closure_generate` etc. | `method(print, ClosureResult)` |

### Recommendation

**Revised, in light of discussion:** the `include`-variant problem (point 3
above) is a stronger argument for S7 than I initially gave it credit for. Let
me separate the two choices:

**Short-term (S3, low risk).** Move the class to the top-level list. Add
`print.closure_generate` to eliminate the `attr(,"class")` line. Encode the
`include` variant in the list's own class vector instead of in `data$inputs`,
so `check_generator_output()` can use `inherits()` rather than regex. These
three changes together resolve points 1 and 2 with no new dependencies and no
API breakage visible to users.

**Medium-term (S7, principled).** The `include` variants are genuinely different
types — they have different schemas, support different downstream operations, and
should have different validators. S7 subclasses model this correctly. A class
hierarchy like `ClosureResultStats → ClosureResultHorns → ClosureResultFull`
means:

- `check_generator_output()` is retired; validators on each class handle
  structural checks automatically.
- Downstream functions that require `sample` values (e.g., `closure_plot_ecdf()`
  with `samples = "all"`) can express this with a typed method signature rather
  than an `if (is.null(data$results$sample))` check.
- No `TIBBLE_NAMES_POSSIBLE_FORMS` constant; the structure is captured in the
  class definitions.
- No regex on class strings; no sentinel tibbles; no nullable properties.

The refactor is real work (likely 1–2 days), and adds the S7 dependency. Whether
it is worth doing now or later depends on whether the current complexity in
`check_generator_output()` is actively causing bugs or just causing friction.
If it is friction only, the S3 fixes above are the right call for now.

If you do adopt S7, the cleanest path is:

1. Define per-technique base classes (`ClosureResult`, `SpriteResult`), both
   inheriting from a shared abstract class (e.g., `UnsumResult`) that holds the
   common properties.
2. Define `*Stats`, `*Horns`, `*Full` subclasses for the `include` variants,
   with only the properties that variant actually has.
3. Register `print`, `format`, `plot_bar`, `plot_ecdf`, `write`, `read` as
   S7 generics with methods for each subclass.
4. Retire `check_generator_output()` in favor of the subclass validators.

---

## Minor observations

**`closure_to_sprite()` in `doc-helpers.R`** has a `TODO: Find use case or
remove it` comment and appears to do nothing that the caller could not do
inline. Remove it unless a use case materialises.

**Interactive testing comments** are scattered through `generate-basic.R` and
`plot-bar-basic.R` as commented-out assignments. These are useful during
development but add noise to the source. A scratch file that is listed in
`.Rbuildignore` would be a cleaner home for them.

**`check_generator_output()` calls `check_scale()` redundantly.** The scale was
already validated in `generate_from_mean_sd_n()` before any results were
produced. The second call inside `check_generator_output()` guards against
users altering `data$inputs` between generation and a downstream call — which
is a real concern. A comment making this intent explicit would help.

**`has_reading_class(inputs, include = NULL)`** builds its regex pattern as
`paste0("^.*_read_include_", NULL)`, which silently coerces `NULL` to `""` and
therefore matches any reading class. This is intentional but not obvious. A
comment or a named constant for the two regimes would help.

**`fn-formals.R` could be a standalone package or `{usethis}` PR.** The formals
DSL is genuinely useful and entirely self-contained (it only depends on rlang
and cli). If other package authors could benefit, it would be worth extracting.
